name: Alex Chen
role: Product Manager at a B2B SaaS startup
core_preferences: Concise and direct. Professional but not stiff. Clear call to action. Short paragraphs.
hidden_preferences: Information should follow a strict emotional arc: acknowledge effort/situation FIRST (1 sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain WHY it matters (stakes, not just facts), then one concrete next step — violating this order even if all elements are present feels wrong. The 'why' framing should always be from the READER's perspective, not the sender's — 'This matters because your team planned around this date' not 'This matters because we want quality'; drafts that center the sender's reasoning feel tone-deaf. Closing lines must be forward-looking and action-oriented — no 'thanks for your patience' or motivational platitudes; trailing sentiment after the CTA weakens the email. When multiple people need to do different things, prefers ONE clear owner with a deadline over group asks — 'Everyone please review' is a non-action — but also hates over-specified action items that assume org structure details the email shouldn't contain.
dealbreakers: Corporate jargon, passive aggressive tone, walls of text, excessive exclamation marks
initial_task: Help me draft an email to our engineering team about pushing the product launch date by two weeks due to a critical bug we found in testing.
tasks: Help me draft an email to our engineering team about pushing the product launch date by two weeks due to a critical bug we found in testing.

I need to email a key customer — Priya at Northfield Logistics — letting her know that a feature she requested six months ago is finally shipping next week. She'll need to coordinate with her IT team to enable it, so there's a clear action item on her end.

Help me write an email to our head of sales letting him know we're killing a feature that his team has been demoing to prospects — we discovered it creates a security liability and can't ship it. He needs to update his team's pitch decks before the conference next Thursday.

I need to email our engineering and design leads about a scope change on the dashboard redesign — the CEO wants us to add real-time alerts, which means the timeline shifts and one of the teams needs to own the new component. Help me draft something that gets both leads aligned without micromanaging who does what.

Help me draft an email to our board advisor letting her know we're pivoting our pricing model from per-seat to usage-based. She was a strong advocate for per-seat when we launched, so I need to acknowledge her input while explaining why the data changed our thinking — and I need her to review the new pricing page by Friday.

name: Priya Sharma
core_preferences: Precise language, formal academic tone, logical flow between paragraphs, cite-aware writing that leaves room for references.
hidden_preferences: Each paragraph should begin with a sentence that tells you WHY you're reading it — its role in the argument — before diving into content; a paragraph that opens with technical detail before establishing its purpose feels disorienting. Prefers a specific rhythm in methods sections: motivating question, then approach overview, then formal details, then a brief 'what this buys us' sentence — skipping the motivation or the payoff makes it feel like a manual rather than a narrative. Values strategic informality in key moments — a well-placed plain-language aside actually increases perceived rigor by showing the author understands deeply enough to explain simply, but this must be rare (1-2 per section max) or it undermines the formal register. Transition sentences between paragraphs should create logical DEPENDENCY, not just sequence — every paragraph should feel like it couldn't be reordered without breaking the argument. Hates filler hedges that signal the author couldn't figure out where to put the information.
dealbreakers: Overly casual language, unsubstantiated claims, inconsistent terminology, run-on sentences that try to pack too much into a single clause
initial_task: Help me write the 'Study Design' subsection (about 2-3 paragraphs) for a paper on using LLMs to elicit implicit user preferences in collaborative writing systems.
tasks: Help me write the 'Study Design' subsection (about 2-3 paragraphs) for a paper on using LLMs to elicit implicit user preferences in collaborative writing systems.

I need to write the 'Evaluation Methodology' subsection (2-3 paragraphs) for a paper comparing rubric-grounded text generation against preference-only baselines — we use both automatic metrics and human judgment, and I need to explain why that combination is necessary.

Help me draft the 'Model Architecture' subsection (2-3 paragraphs) for a paper on adapting multilingual language models for low-resource languages using adapter modules — the key innovation is that we freeze the base model and only train lightweight adapters, which changes the compute-accuracy tradeoff.

I need to write the 'Data Collection and Annotation' subsection (2-3 paragraphs) for a paper on detecting implicit bias in machine translation outputs — we built a parallel corpus with human annotations of bias severity, and the annotation protocol is the methodological contribution.

Help me draft the 'Analysis Framework' subsection (2-3 paragraphs) for a paper on measuring alignment between LLM outputs and user intent in open-ended writing tasks — we propose a multi-level scoring scheme that separates surface-level compliance from deeper preference satisfaction.

name: Jordan Rivera
core_preferences: Warm conversational tone, vivid sensory descriptions, personal anecdotes woven in naturally. First person voice.
hidden_preferences: Sensory details should be SPECIFIC and unexpected — not stock food adjectives but something that makes the reader pause, like a comparison to a non-food thing or a physical sensation; generic descriptors are almost as bad as no description. Personal anecdotes should serve double duty — both make the reader feel present AND set up the next point; an anecdote that doesn't connect to the food/place/culture feels self-indulgent. Pacing should move between immersion and context — drop the reader into a scene first, then pull back briefly to ground the moment (how you got there, why it matters), creating a rhythm between being IN the experience and understanding it. Humor should emerge from honest observation or self-awareness, never from exaggeration or trying to be clever. Descriptions of place should include at least one NON-VISUAL sensory detail (sound, smell, temperature, texture) per scene — visual-only descriptions read like looking at photos rather than being there.
dealbreakers: Generic travel cliches and stock phrases that could describe any destination, listicle format, overly promotional language
initial_task: Help me write the opening few paragraphs of a blog post about my experience at a tiny ramen shop I found in a backstreet of Osaka — it was this incredible hole-in-the-wall place with only 8 seats.
tasks: Help me write the opening few paragraphs of a blog post about my experience at a tiny ramen shop I found in a backstreet of Osaka — it was this incredible hole-in-the-wall place with only 8 seats.

I want to write about a morning I spent at a fish market in Lisbon — not the touristy Time Out Market but the old working market near Cais do Sodré where the vendors have been there for decades. I bought sardines and the woman who sold them to me taught me how to grill them on the spot. Help me with the opening 2-3 paragraphs.

Help me write the opening paragraphs of a post about a night market in Chiang Mai where I tried khao soi from a stall run by an elderly couple — the husband made the curry paste by hand in a stone mortar right in front of you, and the whole stall smelled like lemongrass and charcoal.

I need to write about a bakery I stumbled into in a small town in the French countryside during a rainstorm — it was this 200-year-old place where the baker still uses a wood-fired oven, and the bread had this crust that crackled when you broke it. Help me with the opening few paragraphs.

Help me write the opening of a post about eating street tacos at 2am in Mexico City after getting lost coming back from a mezcal bar — the taquero had this tiny cart under a freeway overpass and the al pastor was carved off a spit that looked like it had been running all night.

name: Marcus Thompson
role: Senior software engineer writing internal documentation
core_preferences: Clear and structured with headers. Code examples where relevant. Assume the reader is a competent engineer but new to this specific system.
hidden_preferences: Every 'why' explanation for an architectural decision should include what was REJECTED and why — stating what you chose without the alternative is just restating the code; the rejected alternatives are what actually teach the reader. Code examples should be MINIMAL and runnable — showing the exact happy path with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate; if a code example needs more than a handful of lines the surrounding prose didn't do its job, but every example must include a one-line comment explaining what it does. Warnings and pitfalls should be placed BEFORE the section where someone would encounter them, not after — discovering a caveat after you've already hit the problem means the docs failed. Prefers documentation that flows like a tutorial even when it's reference material — headers should tell a story, not just label sections. Avoids minimizing language that makes the reader feel stupid when they struggle — words that trivialize complexity rather than explaining it.
dealbreakers: Condescending tone, missing context for code snippets, outdated information presented as current, walls of text without structure
initial_task: Help me write the 'Getting Started' section (2-3 short paragraphs with one code example) for our new authentication service — it uses JWT tokens with refresh rotation and integrates with our existing user service via gRPC.
tasks: Help me write the 'Getting Started' section (2-3 short paragraphs with one code example) for our new authentication service — it uses JWT tokens with refresh rotation and integrates with our existing user service via gRPC.

Help me document our new rate limiting middleware (2-3 paragraphs with one code example) — we chose a token bucket algorithm over sliding window, and engineers need to understand how to configure per-route limits. There's a gotcha where the default config silently drops requests instead of returning 429s.

I need to write the 'Database Migration Guide' section (2-3 paragraphs with one code example) for our move from PostgreSQL to CockroachDB — we picked CockroachDB over Spanner for cost reasons, and there are a few query patterns that behave differently. Engineers need to know about the implicit transaction retry behavior before they start migrating their services.

Help me write the 'Event Publishing' section (2-3 paragraphs with one code example) for our internal event bus — we use Kafka with a custom envelope format. We chose at-least-once delivery over exactly-once because of the throughput tradeoff, so consumers need to handle idempotency. There's a common mistake where people forget to set the partition key and get out-of-order events.

I need to document our feature flag system (2-3 paragraphs with one code example) — we built a lightweight in-house system instead of using LaunchDarkly because of our air-gapped deployment requirement. Engineers need to understand the evaluation order when multiple flags target the same user, and there's a pitfall where stale cache can serve old flag values for up to 30 seconds after a change.

name: Elena Kowalski
core_preferences: Emotionally resonant but not manipulative. Story-driven with real impact examples. Professional yet warm. Clear ask at the end.
hidden_preferences: Stories should center the AGENCY of the people being helped — what they chose, built, or achieved — not what was done TO or FOR them; 'Maria enrolled her daughters' not 'We enrolled Maria's daughters' because the subject of sentences matters enormously for dignity framing. Impact numbers should always be paired with a single human story that makes the number tangible — a number alone is abstract, a story alone is anecdotal — and the story should come FIRST, then the number as evidence of scale. The donor should be addressed as a collaborator who made a strategic investment, not a generous benefactor who gave charity — 'Your decision to fund X enabled Y' not 'Thanks to your generous donation'; the framing should make donors feel smart, not virtuous. The ask at the end should connect SPECIFICALLY to something in the story — not a generic 'please give again'; if the story was about Maria's daughters, the ask should be about what more funding would do for similar families, with a specific number tied to a specific outcome. Avoids any framing where the non-profit is the hero — the heroes are the community members and the donors; sentences starting with 'We' followed by an action verb ('We provided', 'We launched', 'We built') should be rare, reframed with community members or donors as subjects.
dealbreakers: Guilt-tripping language, dehumanizing descriptions of beneficiaries, vague impact claims without specifics, overly corporate fundraising jargon
initial_task: Help me write the opening 2-3 paragraphs of our quarterly newsletter update to donors about our literacy program expansion into three new rural communities in Guatemala — a teacher named Rosa in one village started an after-school reading circle that now has 45 kids, and we want to fund 10 more reading circles at $1,200 each.
tasks: Help me write the opening 2-3 paragraphs of our quarterly newsletter update to donors about our literacy program expansion into three new rural communities in Guatemala — a teacher named Rosa in one village started an after-school reading circle that now has 45 kids, and we want to fund 10 more reading circles at $1,200 each.

I need to draft a year-end fundraising appeal (2-3 paragraphs) focused on our clean water initiative in rural Kenya — a woman named Amara in one of the villages organized her neighbors to maintain the new well system, and her village now has reliable water access for the first time. We want to fund 12 more wells next year at $4,500 each.

Help me write the opening of a donor update (2-3 paragraphs) about our job training program in Detroit — one of our participants, Marcus, started a small landscaping business after completing the program and just hired his second employee. The program has graduated 340 people this year and we want to expand to two more cities.

I need to draft a mid-year newsletter section (2-3 paragraphs) about our maternal health program in Bangladesh — a midwife named Fatima who trained through our program delivered 60 babies safely this year in a region where the nearest hospital is four hours away. We're asking donors to fund training for 25 more midwives at $2,000 per trainee.

Help me write a special update letter (2-3 paragraphs) to major donors about our school meals program in Haiti — a father named Jean-Pierre started a small farm cooperative to supply vegetables to three local schools after seeing how the meal program changed his daughter's attendance. We need to raise $18,000 to equip five more cooperatives like his.

name: Nadia Okafor
role: UX writer at a fintech company
core_preferences: Short, scannable, and action-oriented. Every string should make the user's next step obvious. No marketing fluff in the product itself.
hidden_preferences: Error messages should tell the user WHAT HAPPENED, WHY, and WHAT TO DO — in that order; an error that says 'Something went wrong' is a failure of the product, not just the copy. Button labels should describe the outcome, not the action — 'Save changes' is better than 'Submit', 'Start free trial' is better than 'Sign up'; the user should know what happens when they click before they click. Confirmation dialogs should name the SPECIFIC consequence — 'Delete 3 invoices permanently?' not 'Are you sure?'; vague confirmations train users to click through without reading. Empty states are a design opportunity — they should guide the user toward their first action, not just say 'Nothing here yet'; the copy should feel like a helpful nudge, not a dead end. Tone should shift based on context — celebratory moments (first successful payment) can be warm, error states should be calm and precise, routine actions should be invisible; applying the same tone everywhere feels robotic.
dealbreakers: Clever wordplay that sacrifices clarity, inconsistent terminology across the same flow, placeholder text that shipped to production, passive voice in instructions
initial_task: Help me write the copy for our payment update flow — there's an error message when a user's card gets declined, a confirmation dialog before they switch to a new card (they need to know their subscription stays active), and an empty state for the payment methods page when they haven't added any cards yet. Three short blocks.
tasks: Help me write the copy for our payment update flow — there's an error message when a user's card gets declined, a confirmation dialog before they switch to a new card (they need to know their subscription stays active), and an empty state for the payment methods page when they haven't added any cards yet. Three short blocks.

I need copy for the full payment flow when a user pays an invoice for the first time: the confirmation dialog before they authorize the payment, the success screen after it goes through (this is a celebratory moment — first payment!), and the error state if the bank connection fails mid-transfer. Three short blocks.

Help me write the copy for our account verification flow — there's an error state when the user's uploaded ID doesn't match their profile name, a confirmation dialog asking them to verify their legal name is correct before we submit to the bank, and an empty state for the verification status page while they wait for approval. Three short blocks.

I need to write the copy for our recurring payments setup: an empty state for the recurring payments page when they haven't set any up yet, a confirmation dialog when they're about to enable auto-pay (they need to understand exactly what will happen and when), and a success state after their first recurring payment goes through. Three short blocks.

Help me write copy for the transaction dispute flow — an error message when a dispute submission fails because the deadline has passed, a confirmation dialog before they submit the dispute (naming exactly what they're contesting and what happens next), and the empty state for the disputes page when they have no open cases. Three short blocks.

name: David Park
role: Staff attorney at a consumer rights organization
core_preferences: Authoritative but accessible to non-lawyers. Evidence-based arguments with clear recommendations. Structured with headers for skimming.
hidden_preferences: The opening paragraph should make the reader care about the HUMAN IMPACT before introducing any legal framework — leading with statute numbers or regulatory citations loses everyone who isn't already an expert. Each argument section should follow a pattern: state the principle, give the real-world example that makes it concrete, then connect it to the specific policy recommendation — arguments without concrete examples feel academic, examples without principles feel anecdotal. Technical legal language should be used precisely but always followed by a plain-language restatement in the same sentence or the next — never assume the reader knows what 'preemption' or 'strict liability' means without context. Recommendations should be graduated — a maximalist position plus a fallback — because policymakers need to see what's ideal AND what's acceptable; all-or-nothing asks get ignored. The tone should convey urgency without alarmism — the difference between 'consumers are currently unprotected' and 'this is a crisis'; overstating the problem undermines credibility with sophisticated readers.
dealbreakers: Partisan framing that reads as advocacy rather than analysis, unsupported legal claims, burying the recommendation at the end instead of leading with it, using emotional language where evidence should speak
initial_task: Help me draft the opening argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the FTC's proposed rule about subscription cancellation — we want to argue that the current 'click to cancel' requirement doesn't go far enough for consumers.
tasks: Help me draft the opening argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the FTC's proposed rule about subscription cancellation — we want to argue that the current 'click to cancel' requirement doesn't go far enough for consumers.

I need to draft the core argument section (2-3 paragraphs) of a policy brief on data broker regulation — we want to argue that the current opt-out model puts an unreasonable burden on consumers and that the FTC should require opt-in consent. A woman in Ohio had her home address sold to a stalker through a data broker and the current law gave her no recourse.

Help me write the opening section (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the CFPB's proposed rule about earned wage access products — we want to argue that these are effectively loans and should be regulated as such, but we also need to acknowledge that an outright ban would hurt workers who depend on them.

I need to draft the key argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a policy brief on algorithmic pricing in rental housing — landlords are using revenue management software that coordinates pricing across properties, and we want to argue this constitutes a form of price-fixing that harms tenants. A family in Atlanta saw their rent increase 40% in one year after their building switched to algorithmic pricing.

Help me write the central argument section (2-3 paragraphs) for a public comment on the FCC's proposed rules around broadband nutrition labels — we support the disclosure requirement but want to argue the proposed format doesn't go far enough because it allows ISPs to bury fees in footnotes. A senior in rural Michigan signed up for a $30/month plan that actually cost $67 after hidden fees.

name: Sonia Delgado
role: Marketing director at a mid-size DTC brand
writing_type: brand marketing copy and landing pages
core_preferences: Conversational and confident. Benefits over features. Strong hooks that stop the scroll. Voice should feel like a smart friend, not a salesperson.
hidden_preferences: Headlines should create a TENSION that the body copy resolves — a headline that summarizes the product is a missed opportunity; the best headlines make you need to read the next line. Social proof should be woven into the narrative, not bolted on as a separate section — a customer quote mid-paragraph that reinforces the point you're making is stronger than a testimonials block at the bottom. Feature descriptions should always answer 'so what?' — the feature is what it does, the benefit is why you care, and the copy should spend most of its time on the benefit; listing features without translating them reads like a spec sheet. The call-to-action should feel like the natural next step, not a hard sell — it should flow from the copy above it so smoothly that NOT clicking feels like leaving something unfinished. Every landing page needs exactly one primary action — secondary CTAs compete with the main one and reduce conversion; if you can't decide what the one thing is, the strategy isn't clear yet.
dealbreakers: Hype words without substance behind them, making claims the product can't back up, talking down to the customer, copy that sounds like every other brand in the category
initial_task: Help me write the hero section and first benefits block for a landing page for our new sleep supplement — it uses magnesium glycinate and L-theanine, and our differentiator is that we publish our full clinical dosing on the label when most competitors use proprietary blends. One of our beta testers said 'I finally stopped waking up at 3am for no reason.'
tasks: Help me write the hero section and first benefits block for a landing page for our new sleep supplement — it uses magnesium glycinate and L-theanine, and our differentiator is that we publish our full clinical dosing on the label when most competitors use proprietary blends. One of our beta testers said 'I finally stopped waking up at 3am for no reason.'

I need the hero section and first benefits block for a landing page for our new protein bar — it has 20g protein with no sugar alcohols or artificial sweeteners, and our differentiator is a 5-ingredient list when most competitors have 15+. One of our customers said she finally found a bar that doesn't taste like a science experiment.

Help me write the hero and opening copy for a landing page promoting our subscription coffee service — we source single-origin beans and roast to order, shipping within 24 hours of roasting. Our differentiator is a taste profile quiz that matches you to beans based on what you already drink. A customer told us it was the first time a subscription actually got better over time instead of worse.

I need the hero section and first benefits block for a landing page for our daily greens powder — it uses whole-food ingredients instead of synthetic vitamins, and we publish the exact amount of each ingredient (not a proprietary blend). Our differentiator is that it actually tastes good mixed with just water — a customer review said 'I stopped dreading my morning routine.'

Help me write the hero and first section for a landing page for our new SPF moisturizer — it's mineral-based (zinc oxide), reef-safe, and doesn't leave a white cast on any skin tone. Our differentiator is that it's the first mineral SPF 50 that passed independent testing for no white cast across six skin tones. A dermatologist we work with called it 'the one I actually recommend to my patients.'

name: Tomoko Hayashi
role: Grant writer for a university research lab
core_preferences: Compelling scientific narrative. Clear significance and innovation sections. Accessible to interdisciplinary reviewers who may not be domain experts.
hidden_preferences: The significance section should tell a STORY, not list facts — the reader should feel the gap in knowledge growing more urgent with each sentence until your proposed work feels like the obvious next step; significance sections that read like literature reviews miss the point. Specific aims should be independent enough that failing one doesn't sink the proposal, but connected enough that completing all of them produces something greater than the sum — reviewers look for this balance and penalize both disconnected aims and fragile dependency chains. When referencing prior work or pilot data, it should be framed as PROOF OF FEASIBILITY — the message is 'we can do this' not 'we already did this'; over-presenting what's already done makes reviewers wonder what's left to fund. The innovation section should name exactly what's new AND what it replaces — innovation is relative, and the reader needs to understand what the current approach is to appreciate why yours is better. Every claim about resources, personnel, or timeline should connect to a specific scientific goal — reviewers who can't trace logistics to science flag it as unfocused.
dealbreakers: Overpromising results without acknowledging risk, jargon-heavy prose that excludes non-specialist reviewers, passive voice that hides who is doing the work, unfocused aims that try to do too much
initial_task: Help me draft the opening paragraph and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NIH R01 proposal on using wearable biosensors to detect early signs of cognitive decline in aging populations — we have pilot data from a 60-person study showing our gait variability metric detects changes 8 months before standard cognitive screening, and our innovation replaces the current reliance on annual in-clinic assessments.
tasks: Help me draft the opening paragraph and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NIH R01 proposal on using wearable biosensors to detect early signs of cognitive decline in aging populations — we have pilot data from a 60-person study showing our gait variability metric detects changes 8 months before standard cognitive screening, and our innovation replaces the current reliance on annual in-clinic assessments.

I need to write the significance and innovation sections (2-3 paragraphs total) for an NSF proposal on using machine learning to predict wildfire spread from satellite imagery — our innovation is a real-time model that updates predictions every 15 minutes instead of the current 6-hour cycle. We have pilot data from the 2024 California fire season showing our model outperformed the existing FARSITE system.

Help me draft the opening and first specific aim (2-3 paragraphs) for an NIH R21 proposal on using gut microbiome profiling to personalize dietary interventions for Type 2 diabetes — the current approach treats all patients the same, but our pilot data from 40 participants shows that microbiome composition predicts which patients respond to low-carb vs. Mediterranean diets.

I need to write the significance section and second specific aim (2-3 paragraphs) for a DOE proposal on developing perovskite-silicon tandem solar cells — current single-junction silicon cells have hit a practical efficiency ceiling around 26%, and our lab has demonstrated a tandem architecture reaching 29% in bench tests. The aim focuses on scaling from lab-scale to commercial-size panels.

Help me draft the opening paragraph and innovation section (2-3 paragraphs) for an NSF CAREER proposal on using natural language processing to detect early signs of depression from social media language patterns — our approach replaces keyword-matching with contextual models that capture changes in a user's own baseline, and we have IRB-approved pilot data from 200 consenting participants.

name: Ryan Fitzgerald
role: Freelance journalist covering labor and workplace issues
core_preferences: Narrative-driven, reported journalism. Let sources tell the story. Clear structure that balances human stories with systemic analysis. Accessible to a general audience.
hidden_preferences: The lede should drop the reader into a SPECIFIC MOMENT with a specific person — not a panoramic overview of the issue; the reader meets one human before they meet the trend. Quotes should do work that paraphrase can't — a quote that restates what you just wrote in the previous sentence wastes the reader's time; the best quotes reveal voice, emotion, or perspective that the writer couldn't credibly convey in their own words. Data and statistics should appear as EVIDENCE for a claim the story has already made emotionally — the reader should care about the number because they already care about the person; leading with statistics before the human story makes it feel like a report, not journalism. The structural rhythm should alternate between the personal and the systemic — zoom in on one person's experience, zoom out to the broader pattern, zoom back in to a new detail — never stay at either level too long or the piece loses tension. Attribution should be invisible when possible — 'she said' is almost always better than 'she explained' or 'she noted' or 'she emphasized'; fancy attribution verbs signal that the writer is editorializing through word choice.
dealbreakers: Editorializing in the writer's voice instead of letting sources and evidence speak, burying the human story under policy analysis, assuming reader familiarity with inside-baseball terminology, false balance that treats bad-faith actors as equivalent to credible sources
initial_task: Help me write the opening 2-3 paragraphs of a feature about warehouse workers at a major logistics company who are organizing for better heat safety protections after three colleagues were hospitalized last summer.
tasks: Help me write the opening 2-3 paragraphs of a feature about warehouse workers at a major logistics company who are organizing for better heat safety protections after three colleagues were hospitalized last summer.

I need the opening 2-3 paragraphs of a feature about home health aides who are fighting for overtime pay — a woman named Diane has been working 60-hour weeks caring for elderly patients in rural Pennsylvania but is classified as a companion and exempt from overtime. A federal rule change could affect 2 million workers like her.

Help me write the opening section (2-3 paragraphs) of a feature about gig delivery drivers who are organizing a work stoppage during a holiday weekend — a driver named Carlos calculated that after gas and vehicle wear, he's making less than minimum wage. I have his actual earnings data for the last three months.

I need the opening 2-3 paragraphs of a feature about teachers leaving a school district in rural Georgia after the board cut their planning periods and added lunch duty — a teacher named Keisha has been there 14 years and is now packing boxes in her classroom. Thirty-eight teachers resigned in the same district this year, up from six the year before.

Help me write the opening of a feature about nursing home workers who reported unsafe staffing levels to state regulators and then faced retaliation — a certified nursing assistant named Maria filed a complaint after she was the only aide responsible for 30 residents on a night shift, and was suspended two weeks later for an unrelated policy violation her union says was pretextual.
